votes.reason: The Python code implements the exact same ASCII range checks and arithmetic transformations for case flipping as the Coq code, and the string processing logic is structurally equivalent.
The Python code accurately implements the Coq logic for flipping the case of characters in a string, using equivalent ASCII value manipulations.
The Python code correctly implements the string case-flipping logic defined in the Coq specification, matching the character-by-character ASCII transformations.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_lower checks ASCII 97-122, is_upper checks ASCII 65-90, flip_char converts lowercase to uppercase by subtracting 32 and vice versa by adding 32, flip_case_model applies flip_char to each character, and flip_case_spec checks if the result equals the model output. The implementations produce the same concrete results for all inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_lower checks ASCII 97-122, is_upper checks ASCII 65-90, flip_char converts lowercase to uppercase by subtracting 32 and vice versa by adding 32, flip_case_model applies flip_char to each character, and flip_case_spec checks if the result equals the model output. The implementations produce the same concrete results for all inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_lower checks ASCII 97-122, is_upper checks ASCII 65-90, flip_char converts lowercase to uppercase by subtracting 32 and vice versa by adding 32, flip_case_model applies flip_char to each character, and flip_case_spec checks if res equals the flipped string. The Python uses ord/chr instead of nat_of_ascii/ascii_of_nat but produces the same results for all concrete string inputs.
Python mirrors the Coq: it flips ASCII letters by ±32, leaves other chars unchanged, processes strings elementwise, and the spec checks equality with the model.
Both implement ASCII case flip by checking 'a'-'z' and 'A'-'Z' with ±32 offsets, leave other chars unchanged, map over the string, and the spec is equality to the model.

Both flip characters only for ASCII A-Z/a-z by adding/subtracting 32 and leave others unchanged; strings are processed characterwise; the spec is equality with the model, which the Python code returns as a boolean. Behavior matches for all concrete inputs in the Coq domain.
